segments.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
If you'd like to be able to listen to the show without ads and have full access to bonus content, that's an option.

Well, that was interesting.
I apologize about that glitched opening to the show.
Earlier on, before I went live with the show, I saw that for some reason the chat was glitching too.
It must be one of those nights, unfortunately, but like I said, nonetheless, I apologize.
Tonight's guest is a Dogman researcher with quite a few interesting credentials.
Number one, he's written two Dogman-specific books, two interesting books that I'm sure you're going to want to read.
And in the description for tonight's show, of course, I'm going to post links to those on Amazon for you.

But also, not only that, he's a writer and producer for the Small Town Monsters films that I'm sure you've most likely seen on YouTube and in other places as well, where they have three videos that are dedicated to Dogman, from what I understand.
Well, I can't do this man any justice, so without any further ado, let's bring him in here now.
Tonight's guest is Aaron Deese. Aaron, welcome to the show.
Mr. Cundiff, how are you? Thanks for having me, sir.
I'm doing better than I deserve. How about yourself?
About the same, about the same. That's a good description. I like that.
Well, it's great having you. I obviously really appreciate you being here.
Aaron, please do me a favor and give us a brief bio on yourself.
Yeah, of course. My name's Aaron Deese. I am a San Antonio-based author and researcher.
I'm also a producer and a writer for Small Town Monsters.

And if folks aren't familiar with STM, we're a documentary film company, and we just celebrated our 10th anniversary. We're celebrating it right now. And we make films about Dogman, UFOs, Bigfoot, all that fun stuff.

And yeah, I have two books centric to the Dogman phenomenon that are out there and done a little bit of traveling, done quite a bit of interviewing and put in a few miles researching this stuff. So it's something I'm very passionate about. I'm excited to get into it with you.
You must be passionate about it. All the work you've put into it. Yeah. I can't imagine trying to do all that work if you're not.
Right. If something you're not interested in, man, that'd be a, that'd be a bummer. So.
Yeah, sure would be. And you touched on this just a moment ago, just like I did in the opening words. You mentioned how you wrote two Dogman specific books. Please tell us, please tell us about them.

Yeah, sure. So the first was the Texas Dogman Triangle. That was released in 2023. And that book is about exactly what it sounds like. It's about dogman sightings in the state of Texas. Oh, there's Heather Mosier. Heather Mosier is the editor at Small Town Monsters Publishing and was the editor on these books I'm telling you about. So that's exciting. Good timing there.

But yeah, the first book focused on dogman sightings in Texas. And it seems that looking at a map, there's a preponderance of sightings within a geographical area that runs from Dallas-Fort Worth down to San Antonio over to Houston and back again.
And so that's where the term the Texas Dogman Triangle came from.
And then the second book is called Hunting Grounds, Dogman of the Lakes.
And that is about dogman phenomena in the land between the lakes.
And that book was released last year.
So those are the two that are out there right now.
Wow, you do stay busy, don't you?
I get bored otherwise.
Gosh, I have to wonder when you have time to sleep.
Oh, you don't.

I have a two so sleeping is something I said goodbye to a long time ago Yeah I bet you did that two year old keeps you busy also He does Yeah He my own little werewolf Yeah Yeah I can only imagine You mostly focus on dogman happenings in Texas there
It's easy to think if you just are now getting into the whole dogman thing to think that you
might expect to have a dogman encounter in Michigan or Pennsylvania or Ohio, New York,

places like that, Tennessee, Kentucky. But it seems like most people, when they're just getting into this, they just brush right over Texas. They don't think about Texas being a hot spot for dogman encounters. Have you noticed that? Or is it just me?

I have. Yeah. And I think it's gaining more traction. I think in the last couple of years, people are paying more attention to Texas with regard to this phenomenon. And I don't mean, oh, because my book came out. I like to think that I helped, but the Dogman community is vast and they're not all reading my books. You know what I mean? But I do see more chatter. Hey, Al, thank you for that. I appreciate that. It's a very nice comment.
But I do see more chatter in a lot of the online communities and Facebook groups I follow because, you know, I've mentioned this to you before, Vic.
I like to watch the conversation and see what people are saying.
And I do see more conjecture over the state of Texas.
And since my book came out on the topic, I've been fortunate enough to be contacted by several people who said, hey, you know, I've had sightings like this or I've had sightings in this area.
And I didn't realize this was a thing.
I didn't realize other people were seeing these things.
So, you know, it's, I'm hopeful that more people kind of will pay attention to Texas in this regard because there are more sightings, there's more data, there are more reports.
And the more we have on record, the more we stand to learn.
So, you know, I think we're just really getting started as far as like looking at Texas as an area worthy of continued study.
It should be because a lot of encounters happen there.
And with that in mind, why do you think so many dogmen are there apparently?

Man, then we get into what are these things, you know? If you look at it purely from a biological standpoint, the topography in Texas lends itself to most forms of wildlife. Not all of them, but most of them. We had camels here at one time during the Civil War. That was a thing. You can look that up.

You know, we have bears in some areas, we have foxes, we have desert areas and all the animals that go along with that. But then we also have swamp lands and forests. So if these entities or creatures are in any way a biological being, it's not too out of scope to think they could find a place where they would be comfortable in the state of Texas.

We also has vast, vast stretches of unoccupied, unobserved land that may be government land, it may be ranch land, tons of ranches and farms out here, but places where you don't necessarily have a ton of people where something could hide in relative privacy.
So I think just the way the state is, how spread out it is, how many large areas there are, I think that lends itself to sightings of any unidentified creature.
Oh, there's my buddy's hell-bent holler.
Thank you, guys.
Appreciate you.
So I think that is a strong element to it as well.

But then once you get into some of the more, the stranger aspects of this phenomenon and people say, well, you know, these seem to be more than just animals. Are they something supernatural? Then you get into what is it about Texas that lends itself to strange phenomena? Because we've got everything out here. You know, we've got UFOs, ghosts, buried treasure, pirate curses. We've got Bigfoot in spades. I think I said Bigfoot already.
We have some of the most notorious UFO and alien contactee sightings on record come from Texas.
So you could argue that the state itself is just super strange.
But, you know.
Yeah, there are a lot of strange things that have happened there, but it's a big area.
It really is.
As far as a split, does it seem like more encounters happen in the eastern half of the state or the western?
That's a good question.
The Dogman Triangle itself is more kind of central, eastern central sort of bleeding over into the western side.
So I would say that's where the most seem to be.
But once you get further out west, going past like Kerrville, you know, you start running into just really small towns, tiny little towns, 200, 300 people, maybe a thousand people.
You don't have as many folks around to have sightings.

So there certainly is an element of that as you're getting closer to these larger cities like San Antonio, Dallas, Houston. These are some of the largest cities in the country. So of course, there are going to be more smaller towns around them where the environment lends itself to strange creature sightings, if you like, but where there are people to have those sightings.

So you could argue that as you go further out west there just aren as many people But again that one of those things where we just need more data We need more information You make some really good points there Encounters do happen where the people are If there not a person to witness it then yeah there no encounter So that a really good
point. It's obvious from the things that you've shared that you focus on Texas mostly, but you've
put a pretty good amount of work into researching another place called the Land Between the Lakes
area and the dogman activity that's been alleged to happen there. What more can you tell us about
What got you into researching in the LBL?
So if you spend more than 10 minutes researching dogman phenomenon or real werewolf encounters, there are a few things you're going to trip over almost immediately.
And one of them is the alleged land between the lakes incident or land between the lakes massacre, as it's called.
So I had kind of an interest in that just as a story because I love stories.
You know, I think most folks who are interested in this stuff do.
and it wasn't something I treated with a whole lot of seriousness. And then around the end of 2023,
I was just kind of getting involved with small town monsters at a more, you know, active capacity.
And it was decided that, Hey, we're going to look at land between the lakes and we're going to do it
is kind of a unified way. We're going to explore it in as many ways as possible. We're going to
look at the dog man stuff. We're going to look at the Bigfoot stuff. We're going to look at some of
the paranormal stuff. And I was already making notes for another book, you know, still looking
at the dogman phenomenon, kind of building on some specific themes and ideas, just stuff I had
stumbled across while writing the dogman triangle, but that I didn't get the chance to explore fully.
And then I start looking into the land between the lakes and I realized these themes are consistent,
not just in Texas, but also in the LBL. So it really, it happened as a result of this kind of
unified research push that we were doing as a team, which was a super cool thing to be a part of.
But it also kind of happened organically because it was also already on my short list, so to speak.
Yeah, I can understand why it was. And it seems to me like the LBL when it comes to dogmen,
it's kind of like an onion. The more you start removing layers, the more you find.
That is an apt description. Yes, sir.
So not only is it apt, but it's also accurate.
Apt and accurate. Yes, sir.
The double A on that one. A plus plus.
Sorry, couldn't help myself there.
No, that was good.
Do you consider yourself to be a researcher?

Yes, sir. Yeah, that's the term I use most often. I do produce podcasts, so I'll call myself a podcaster. And I do write books, so I can say I'm an author. But I don't consider myself an investigator.
I don't get out there on a regular basis and put boots on the ground like Jesse Joe over at Hellbent Hollerdoo or Alex Petikov over at Small Town Monsters.
You know, those guys are investigators.
I don't consider myself a cryptozoologist because there are people like, you know, Ken Gerhard and Lyle Blackburn who have the zoological credentials to call themselves cryptozoologists.
So for me, I like to say I'm a researcher because that's where everything I do starts.
It all starts with research.
Well, just being honest.
That's always a good thing.
And just a moment ago, you mentioned the fact that you put some research into the alleged murders that took place at the LBL.
What kind of research did you do to try to find out if it actually did happen or not?
So we did everything we could to either confirm or deny the details of that story.
And by we, I mean myself, Heather Mosier, the rest of the SDM crew, Shannon LeGrow, who was on that film shoot with us.

There were sessions during that film shoot. We were in the LBL for about five days that unfortunately were not filmed, but we were all huddled around laptops and phones and tablets looking into certain aspects of this story because as we were spending time in the land between the lakes, talking with locals, interviewing witnesses, we're getting other details and other tidbits that are not a part of the internet version, if you like.
You know, and despite all that, we were not able to find anything that confirms or strongly indicates that that event took place as it is purported.
So that's one answer.
The other answer is that that conclusion leads a lot of people to, OK, well, this is an Internet invention.
This is a creepypasta.
We can trace the origins of this story to the 2007 film Hunt the Dogman High Strangeness in Kentucky.
which was produced by Barton Nunley, noted author, cryptozoologist, you know, producer,
very accomplished guy. And in that film, he interviews a woman named Jan Thompson,
who tells this story. And so for a lot of folks, that's considered the origin point.
Miss Thompson's version, a written version of the story was available on the internet for a few
years before Mr. Nunley produced the film about it. So a lot of folks will read that original
account and go, okay, this is written in very flowery language. Clearly this was just written

as a creative writing project and they sort of write it off and move on What very interesting for me is that there are people in the Land Between the Lakes area such as the town of Grand Rivers a buddy
of mine named Mike Smith, who is quoted in the book and in the film, who heard this story as
early as 1982. Mr. Smith heard it, I believe, in 1987. Elijah Henderson of the Cryptid Studies
Institute is in contact with folks in Grand Rivers who heard it in 1982. Whether it's 82,
87, doesn't matter. Because in either case, we know that this predates internet creepypastas
and internet urban legends. The internet was in its very early forms in the 80s. So if the first
time people can remember hearing this story is the early 80s, it doesn't mean it's true, but it does
mean that writing it off as purely an internet invention is something we can't do because we
know it's been around longer than that. From there, I'm going, okay, well, is there anything
else to the dogman phenomenon in the land between the lakes other than this one really hope high
profile case turns out there is i'm sure i've already seen mr martin groves mentioned in the
chat once uh great dude good friend of mine um he's had some very frightening encounters with
these creatures in the land between the lakes and even going beyond mr groves there are several
independent named witnesses who will tell you i have had encounters with what i believe to be an
upright canine in this area. So I'll say this, and hopefully I don't upset anybody. It is my opinion that the events of the, should I say that now, Vic, or do we want to save that? Sorry.
Oh, no, you're fine. Go ahead and save it.

Okay. I don't want to derail the trajectory of it. It is my opinion that the events of the LBL massacre, as they're purported online, did not occur, simply because we can't find anything to corroborate that, including records of these people, where they're supposed to be from, you know, any way you can think to research this, we've done that. Other people have done it long before I did.
But because we do have legitimate dogman encounters in this area, I'm of the opinion that there is some event at the root of this that led to this story.

And it could be something as mundane as a family was camping in the land between the lakes and one of them was bitten by a coyote or scratched by a dog, you know, and that morphs into, excuse me, that morphs into, oh, they were attacked by a coyote and then they were attacked by a bear.
And, oh, actually, it was a werewolf that comes along with these existing rumors of upright canines in the area.

And that's just my theory. But the story also says a lot about the land between the lakes itself, because if you look at the history of this area, you know, it was a Native American hunting ground before settlers came along in the 1700s.
Many of them led by Daniel Boone, who happens to be a distant relative of mine.
True story. These folks set up shop in the LBL. They were there for generations.

And then in the 1960s, the United States government came in, said, we're turning this into a national recreation area. You guys got to get out. So you have this place where up to 2,000 American families, according to some accounts, were forcibly displaced, the vast majority of them against their will.

And then a few decades later, two decades later, you have this story about an unstoppable force coming in and destroying a family in this same area. And I got to give credit to my friend Eli Watson for this take, because I interviewed him for the book and he presented this perspective.
And I think it's really, really apt because it says something about a real world event, something that happened to real people.
And it's set against the backdrop of this very strange place where these strange encounters really do happen.
So, no, I don't think I know at all.
There's a lot I don't know.
Sorry, I'm just reading the chat here.
So, you know, that's my opinion on this case.
But I'm more than happy to be proven wrong.
If someone can prove me wrong, at least we've proven something.
And I think that's awesome. But that's where I land after reading up on this for, you know, two or so years.

I know you've put a lot of research into it. I don't know, though, Aaron, do you think it's beyond the government's capability to cover up two murders that actually did happen there, but put us where we are today with the lack of evidence?

I don't think it's beyond their capacity. I just think it's unlikely. And the reason I think it's unlikely is because for the events of the massacre to have taken place, you're talking about purportedly dozens of witnesses.
And I'm referencing the original kind of testimony version, the one that most folks think of.
You're talking about dozens of witnesses.
And at some point, once you have enough people involved, someone is going to talk.
And we don't have anybody that has claimed to be a witness of this event.
Now, it's entirely possible that those folks just said, you know what?
Now I'm going to stick to my silence and never go on record.
But we can kind of observe an analog in the UFO world where we do
have an ongoing trend with on the government side of keeping this stuff under wraps,
denying it, saying, no, there's nothing on record. But now we have whistleblowers coming forward and
saying, actually, we do have these things on record. And that's been going on for a long
time as well, at least since the 80s. So I know you can't make a one to one comparison between
the dogman and UFO research communities. And I don't mean to paint with such a broad brush.
But I do feel that we need a little bit more.
You know what I mean?
And again, I'm not going to say this didn't happen.
There's no way it happened.
We just haven't found that smoking gun, at least yet.
Could be out there.
Could be out there.
I see Jonathan Dodd in the chat.
Jonathan Dodd provided the cover art for Texas Dogman Triangle and Hunting Grounds, as well as the little profile icon I'm using here.
Yeah, that's amazing artwork.
I was looking at that before we got underway.
and I'm really impressed.
He does really good work.
He's a legend, man.
Yeah, I can see why you say that.
I really can.
What we were just talking about, bouncing back to that,
I think it's all too easy to underestimate the abilities of the U.S. government,
their reach and their pull, their ability to do things,
to pull certain things off that seem almost impossible.
And I wouldn't put it past them when you consider the resources to bury
two murders that happened there.
I'm not saying that I believe 100% they happened, but there's a lot of evidence out there that, in my opinion, is awfully hard to ignore.
I mean, there have been several people who I know who have gone to the LBL and various people have taken them to the exact same place.
People who didn't know each other have taken them to the exact same places where these murders supposedly happened.
And there have been other things to corroborating evidence that, gosh, it's just really hard to dismiss.
I could be wrong.
Maybe they didn't happen.
But as time has gone by over the years, at first, I just thought it was nonsense.
But as time has gone by, I don't know.
I wouldn't be surprised if they did happen.
And I'm on the other side of the coin there.
And this is part of the challenge in writing a book about this kind of thing, because you want to weigh it from both sides.
you know and my opinion on this will morph over time as well you know it's a tough one because
like you said if it is all nonsense why can't we just write it off why are this many people
convinced about it why does the story have that kind of staying power and that at least in part
is why i do believe there is some event at the root of this you know we may never know all the
details but i do believe that there is a basis to this story in reality so i am definitely with you
there. It's just like you said, there's so many layers you peel back one and you go, okay,
what about this back on the other side, round and round? Yeah. Yeah, definitely. There are a lot of
layers, like you said. Well, from what I understand there, Aaron, you have a relative who told you
about a dogman encounter they had before you tell us about that experience that they shared with you
is that what got you into this whole dogman thing or was it something else?
Yeah, it really is. It really is. I had started a podcast with my wife called Hey, Strangeness
during the pandemic. You know, like I think a lot of us started during the pandemic. You've
been around quite a bit longer, but you know, there's a whole legion of us who came in during
the pandemic. And I was looking for a topic to do just for a general something strange in Texas,
because that's where we live. And I'm having a chat with this person who is a relative who I've
known for many, many years, who says, well, you know, I saw something strange once.
And they relay to me what sounds like a roadside encounter.
And I go, well, you ever heard of this thing called Dogman?
They're like, no, what are you talking about?
So we start talking about the Beast of Bray Road and the Michigan Dogman.
And, you know, this person gets pale in the face and they're like, that looks like what
I saw, you know, looking at the artistic renditions and the eyewitness sketches.
So then I do a little more research and come to find out that there are other reports
within, you know, a hundred miles of this, this random sighting that this person didn't realize
was significant at the time. And that's where the term Texas dog man triangle came from. Cause
there were three that were kind of clustered in this one area. Um, but then of course it grows
as you realize there are more reports and stuff, but sorry, the short answer to your question is,
yes, that was kind of the linchpin that really cinched this for me because it's,
I'm fascinated by this stuff, even just looking at it online, watching documentaries,

listening to interviews. But once you have that personal connection to someone, whether it's your own experience, or even somebody, you know, that grounds it a lot more in reality. And that's kind of what set me on this whole journey, as it were.
It's a slippery slope, isn't it?
It is.

Yeah definitely is Well please tell us about that encounter that they had Yeah So they were driving they were coming from Colorado to Austin actually on their way to visit me and my wife And they were down a long highway I believe it is 281 far far north outside of Austin And you know I hate to use the term atypical but it was kind of what some of us think of as an atypical roadside sighting
They're coming around a long bend.
It's at night.
They're in a fairly isolated area.
So you've got trees on all sides, very wooded space.

And this person sees a large canine form raised up on its rear legs, holding what appears to be roadkill or something like that in its forepaws or front legs, you know, claws, however you'd like to describe it.
And as they're coming around the bend, they had eyes on it for probably 10 to 15 seconds.
As they come around the bend and get a little closer, it stands up on its rear legs, turns and starts walking into the underbrush.
This person is a veteran of the conflict in Afghanistan.
They've been in dangerous situations.
They know how to respond, how to observe, you know, and how to report on what they've seen.
They are a business owner.
They hold a real estate license in two states.
You know, and I say this stuff because very often people call witness credibility into question.
But for me, even just this person going beyond being a family member, it's like you've got all these credentials as well, dude.
Like if you went loud with this story on the Internet or something, it could destroy your reputation and ruin your business.
So the fact that it was being relayed to me in private by someone I know, you know, that all the more that just made it all the more impactful.
I'm sure it did. I'm sure it did.

It's one thing when you get into this and you don't know anyone that has had any experiences, but when you find out that they have, when families had experiences or close friends have, that really changes it.
It really just pulls out the realism and just does something to you.
It really does.
This is something that you don't know about, but I put this out there, I think, what, two weeks ago?
my copyright attorney he told me he contacted me to let me know that a family member of his
has had a dogman encounter then he went on to tell me about the encounter what happened and
everything but yeah that blew me away more people and this is something that i tell eyewitnesses all
the time i'll tell them you probably know people more than one person who's had an encounter with
one of these things with dogmen but you don't know it because they haven't put it out there
and told you or other people about it for obvious reasons too.
But yeah, it's something else.
I wasn't expecting that.
That's wild.
Yeah, that's the thing with a lot of these encounters, Vic.
I think people see these things and they figure,
oh, that was just a weird dog.
Well, maybe some of them are weird dogs.
But then when you take into account dozens to hundreds of eyewitness sightings
of weird dogs walking on two legs,
there's something else going on here.
That's my opinion.
Oh, definitely.
You didn't directly address it, but what you just said, it brought another question to mind, and this is a juicy one.

Why do you think it is that there are so many people who are cryptid connoisseurs, I guess you could say, who wholeheartedly believe in the existence of Sasquatch, even though they've never seen one, but they poo-poo the idea that there could possibly be such a thing as a dogman out there?
Yeah, I love this question.
I've got the biggest smile on my face.
I think there are a couple of reasons for it.

One, probably the most prominent one, is the association with pop culture. And that's inseparable. We're never going to get away from that. The fact that people use the term werewolf as such a common descriptor kind of speaks to that.
you know, when you have this image that's so deeply ingrained in pop culture and has been
for going on a century now, that's natural. It's natural for people to immediately hear
Dogman slash Werewolf and go, this is nonsense. We already have Bigfoot. We already have the
Flatwoods monster. We don't need to, to bring something else in. And I say that because that
was my perspective on it when I first encountered this phenomenon. Before I read the works of Linda
Godfrey, that was my attitude. You know, once I read a couple of her books, I was like, oh,
okay, there's more to this. But I think that's a big reason. What a lot of folks don't account for
is the fact that human beings all over the world considered werewolves real to be an actuality for
a very, very long time. And when I say werewolf, I don't mean the Hollywood transform under a full
Moon version. I mean, some iteration of the upright canine slash human chimeric motif. That's
been around with us since ancient Mesopotamia, you know. So, and that's not my opinion. That's just a
fact. I'm sure a lot of folks listening or in the audience have read Sabine Barr and Gould's Book of

Werewolves That was published in 1857 and the stories in that book are much much older Thank Thank you Glenn I appreciate that So people for a long time have considered this to be a legitimate phenomenon
But then once the movies came along and borrowed from a lot of these old folk stories and these old tales, that's when it became thought of as something that was purely of pop culture.
So there's that.
And then on the other side, it's difficult for a lot of people to stomach the idea of any kind of undiscovered, upright, six to eight foot tall creature.
and I'll quote Lyle Blackburn on this one. It's even harder to stomach the idea of more than one.
So when you get into, okay, could Bigfoot be a thing? Yeah, sure. Bigfoot could be a thing.
Apes are real. Oh, but there's also werewolves. I think it just kind of becomes too much.
And I don't mean to patronize or condescend because again, this was very much my perspective
for quite a long time. But when you take into account the historical record, the folkloric
precedent for people believing in these things. And then you put that alongside these modern encounters, which, you know,
Vic, you're over 550 episodes. As you know, there are hundreds of them. For me, it's very difficult to go, oh, well,
people are just misidentifying Bigfoot or, oh, this couldn't possibly be a thing. And then just as a side note, dogs can
walk on two legs. They don't do so in natural situations unless given a very, very good reason, but they do possess that
ability. So I don't know. You raised some really good points. It's funny, Aaron, I didn't expect to
necessarily blindside you with that question, but I didn't think you'd put as much thought into this
as you obviously have. You have put a lot of thought into this, it sounds like. I do think about it quite a bit,
yeah. And part of it's just, you know, following the discussion and seeing how many people are very
skeptical of the phenomenon. And again, understanding that skepticism, you know, the,
all my family and friends know what I do for a living, but 90% of them don't, don't think real
werewolves are a thing. That's okay. That's fine. I'm more concerned about, you know, hearing the
stories of people that have actually experienced this phenomenon and giving them a voice when I
have the opportunity to, but yeah, yeah. Well, it's good that you do that. It's obviously
you have an open mind. And as long as you have an open mind, that's a good thing in my opinion.
